turns.content: Hey, so I've got a working PoC where my Rust code calls browser APIs via js_sys::Promise. It feels clunky though, like I'm manually wrapping every callback just to handle the async flow.

That makes sense given your scripting background; in many languages you might have used a promise wrapper or an event loop directly, but Rust doesn't really need that manual wrapping when using wasm-bindgen-futures. The goal is to let the compiler handle the async conversion for us instead.
Right, so I'm trying to access DOM elements from web_sys::Window and then trigger an action that needs to be asynchronous. Can you show me how to do this properly?

Sure thing; you'd want to use web_sys::Window for the context, but remember that DOM access itself is synchronous in Rust unless you're actually waiting on a browser event. The async part comes from converting your JavaScript callback into an awaitable future using wasm-bindgen-futures.
Got it, so I'm mixing js_sys::Promise with web_sys::Window? That sounds like a lot of boilerplate. Is there a simpler way to structure this?

You're right; you can avoid the manual promise wrapping by letting wasm-bindgen-futures handle it, but be careful not to confuse js_sys::Promise with web_sys::Window. The former is for JS promises in general, while the latter is specifically the browser window object.
Okay, so I need to feature-gate web-sys? Should I add a flag like cfg(feature = "web-sys") everywhere?

Yes, exactly; you should feature-gate web-sys in your Cargo.toml so it only gets included when needed. This keeps your build size smaller and helps with tree-shaking if you're targeting production environments.
What about concurrency? If I'm doing multiple async operations, do I need to manage a runtime manually?

Not really; wasm-bindgen-futures provides the necessary bridge between Rust futures and JavaScript promises. You don't have to set up your own event loop unless you're doing something very advanced with WebAssembly's multi-threading features.
I see, so I can just use the async/await syntax here? But what if I need to access a specific JS object like console.log?

You would typically call those through js_sys::Value or web_sys::console, but since you're already using wasm-bindgen-futures for async, just make sure your Rust function returns a Future and the JS side handles the promise.
One more thing; I noticed some people use js_sys::Value for generic data. Is that better than web_sys::Window for this?

js_sys::Value is great if you need to pass arbitrary JS values around, but web_sys::Window is specifically what you want when interacting with the browser context like document.body or window.innerWidth.
Thanks! So my next step is wrapping this in a proper crate with feature flags. One last question; how do I handle errors from these calls?
You can use wasm_bindgen::JsCast or convert the Result<T, JsError> into a Rust error type like anyhow::Error or std::io::Error. Just be careful to propagate any JS exceptions through your async flow.
I'm reading the book on my first day at this bootcamp job, and it's confusing me. What's the difference between js-sys and web-sys?

That is a great question to start with, but you're actually missing a key distinction here. The book isn't really about two separate crates; it's about how wasm-bindgen handles different APIs. js_sys is the core set of bindings for JavaScript types like arrays and strings that are fundamental to every JS engine. web_sys, on the other hand, is specifically designed to expose browser-specific APIs like DOM elements and the Window object. The real difference isn't just in what they contain, but in how you use them together.
Ah, okay. So I can just pick whichever one I want? Like if I need a Promise I should grab it from js_sys?

You are making the wrong assumption there. While js_sys does have basic types like arrays and strings, you cannot actually find a native Promise type in js_sys that works for your use case. If you try to access js_sys::Promise directly in a real browser environment without extra configuration, it will fail because those bindings aren't present by default. You need the specific feature-gating for web-sys to get the full set of browser APIs including Promises.
Wait, so I have to add a feature flag? Is that just an optional setting?

Exactly. You absolutely need to enable the web-sys feature when building your crate. Without it, you are missing critical browser-specific types like web_sys::Window and web_sys::Document. This is why the exercises in your book always show both crates being imported together; they represent a unified view of the browser environment from Rust's perspective.
I see, so I can't just use js_sys for everything like normal JS?

Right. In standard JavaScript you can grab anything from the global scope, but in Rust you need explicit bindings. If you want to interact with the DOM or handle browser events properly, web_sys is your go-to for those things. js_sys is more like a minimal toolkit for generic JS data structures.
Okay, so how does async work in this setup? I've been struggling with Promises in the exercises.

That's a great question. The key is using wasm-bindgen-futures. When you call web_sys::Promise methods, they return futures that need to be awaited properly. You can't just use regular async/await without the right crate because wasm-bindgen needs special handling for asynchronous operations in the browser context.
So if I try to await a Promise from js_sys directly, it won't work?
Correct. Since there's no native Promise in js_sys that matches the browser's behavior, you must rely on web_sys::Promise combined with wasm-bindgen-futures to get proper async support.
This is getting frustrating because I thought I was learning new stuff but it feels like more boilerplate. Is there a way to avoid all this?

I completely understand your frustration with the complexity right now. You're not alone in feeling that way when starting out. The reality is that Rust's type safety and strict compilation requirements mean you can't just drop into whatever JS APIs you need without proper setup. But once you get used to the pattern of importing web_sys and using wasm-bindgen-futures, it becomes much more predictable than standard JavaScript.
You're right, I guess. So for a simple exercise like this, what's the best way to structure my code?

For that specific book exercise, you should import both crates at the top: use wasm_bindgen::prelude::*; use js_sys::{Array, String}; and then add the web-sys feature to your Cargo.toml. Then in your main function, you can safely call web_sys::Promise methods knowing they will resolve correctly with wasm-bindgen-futures.
One last thing - if I want to access the Window object specifically?
That's where web_sys::Window comes in handy. It gives you direct access to window-level operations like document or location properties, which js_sys simply cannot provide on its own.

Why does this even work this way? I thought Rust was all about compile-time safety, so why do I have to manually wrap a browser Promise with wasm_bindgen::JsFuture and then use the future_to_cow or async block pattern just to get something that feels like async in my crate?

The reason is that JavaScript's async/await model runs on a single-threaded event loop, while Rust is multi-threaded and eager. When you call into JS from Rust via js_sys::Promise or web_sys::Window methods, the result isn't immediately available to your thread; it sits in the browser until the next microtask tick. That's why wasm-bindgen-futures exists as a crate specifically to bridge that gap by converting those JS futures into Rust futures you can actually wait on from within your code.
Okay, so I'm using js_sys::Promise for the promise object itself but I need web_sys::Window for some DOM manipulation right? Can't I just use js_sys directly everywhere?

You can access basic values like strings or numbers via js_sys, but any operation that touches the browser environment—like getting the document body or a specific element by id—requires web_sys because those are exposed as JavaScript objects. For example, to get the DOM root you'd use web_sys::Window::document and then call methods on those DOM nodes which aren't available in js_sys.

Got it, but what about feature gating? I'm building a proof-of-concept so I want to avoid bundling everything if the browser doesn't support WebAssembly. How do I ensure my crate only compiles when WASM is actually needed?

You use cfg-features in your Cargo.toml and then guard imports with #[cfg(feature = "wasm")]. For example, if you want to include web_sys or js-sys only when the wasm feature is enabled, you'd wrap those dependencies like this: [dependencies.web-sys = { version = "0.3", features = ["document"] }] and then use conditional compilation so your code doesn't try to reference undefined traits in a non-WASM build.
So if I forget the feature flag, my entire app will fail to compile? That seems harsh for a proof-of-concept.

Yes, it will. If you try to use web_sys without enabling its features in your build configuration, the compiler won't find the types you're trying to reference and will error out immediately. That's exactly why feature-gating is important—it lets you disable heavy dependencies like this crate for faster compilation or smaller binaries when they aren't needed.
I see. One more thing, I'm trying to implement an async function that reads from the DOM and updates a counter. Should I use std::future or something else?

You should definitely use wasm-bindgen-futures here because it provides the correct integration with Rust's async ecosystem when dealing with JS futures. Specifically, you'd convert your js_sys::Promise into a future using the wasm_bindgen_futures::JsFuture::from_promise method so you can then use .await in your standard async context without breaking type safety.
That makes sense. So my function signature would be something like fn update_counter() -> impl Future<Output = ()> where the body uses that conversion?

Exactly, and you might want to return a concrete type instead of an opaque trait for better error handling. For instance, returning Result<(), JsValue> or some specific error enum defined in your crate so callers can inspect what went wrong if the DOM operation fails.
What about error propagation? Can I just use standard Rust errors and have wasm-bindgen handle the conversion?

You can define your own custom error type that includes a JsValue field to store any JavaScript error returned by the browser, then implement std::error::Error for it so you can propagate Rust errors alongside JS failures. This way your callers get familiar Rust ergonomics while still having access to the actual JS error data.
One last thing—I'm curious about the difference between js_sys and web_sys beyond what we've covered so far. Is there anything else I should know?

js_sys is focused on primitive JS types like strings, numbers, arrays, and functions that don't require a window context. web_sys adds the full DOM environment including elements, events, and timing APIs like requestAnimationFrame or performance.now(). You can think of js_sys as the low-level bindings and web_sys as the high-level browser interface layer.

Thanks! I think I have a clear picture now. Just to confirm, my final setup will be wasm-bindgen-futures for async, web_sys for DOM, feature-gated in Cargo.toml, and standard Rust error types with custom fields for JS errors?

That's perfect. You'll end up with a clean architecture where your Rust code handles logic and state while delegating to the browser via web_sys for DOM interactions, using wasm-bindgen-futures to make those interactions truly asynchronous from Rust's perspective.
